indicators.rubric: 0 = BoJ fully autonomous, no government language on rate path; 1 = government language nudges BoJ guidance or seeks GPIF rotation without formal mandate change; 2 = formal mandate change, government veto over rates, or snap election driven by monetary policy

indicators.label: US Fed independence stress
indicators.rubric: 0 = Fed chair credibly independent, forward guidance intact; 1 = forward guidance removed or chair under documented White House pressure; 2 = formal attempt to remove chair, Congressional override of Fed mandate, or rate decision reversed under political pressure

indicators.label: G7 central bank policy divergence (policy rate range, bps)
indicators.rubric: Range of G7 policy rates in bps; widening beyond 400bps signals disorderly divergence that strains FX and carry structures

new-risk-dm-central-bank-independence-un.rationale: No existing story captures the simultaneous political-interference dimension across both the US Fed and BoJ in the same reporting week. This is distinct from s2 (which covers Japan fiscal/JGB/carry mechanics) and s1 (Treasury basis trade) — it is about the credibility anchor itself. If both the world's two largest sovereign bond market central banks are perceived as politically captured in a high-debt/high-inflation environment, the tail risk is a simultaneous repricing of term premia in USTs and JGBs, which would cascade through s1, s2, s4, and s14. The IMF WEO explicitly warns that 'eroded policy buffers can amplify' downside risks. Proposed level 6 reflects that political language has been partly walked back but the structural risk is now observable and documented.

japan-government-floats-gpif-repatriatio.headline: Japan government floats GPIF repatriation of foreign assets to support JGB market — systemic cross-asset unwind risk
japan-government-floats-gpif-repatriatio.summary: Finance Minister Katayama publicly signaled on July 10 that Japan would 'pursue measures to encourage the GPIF and other pension funds to invest more in Japanese financial assets,' prompting market participants to assume a large-scale reallocation from foreign bonds/equities into JGBs — a potential forced unwind of the world's largest pension fund's foreign holdings.

proposed_indicator.label: GPIF foreign-asset repatriation signal (0 none / 1 verbal-only / 2 policy-mandated reallocation)
proposed_indicator.rubric: 0 = no government pressure on GPIF asset mix; 1 = verbal encouragement by Finance Minister or MoF with no formal mandate change (current state as of 2026-07-10); 2 = formal revision to GPIF mid-term investment objectives or ministerial directive mandating increased domestic allocation. A move to 2 would signal forced selling of foreign Treasuries, equities, and currency, with systemic cross-asset implications (US Treasuries, USD, global equities).

japan-government-floats-gpif-repatriatio.rationale: The existing s2_japan story captures JGB yields, USD/JPY, and Takaichi financing clarity — but none of its indicators captures the specific and novel risk of a GPIF repatriation-driven global asset unwind. The GPIF manages approximately $1.5 trillion in assets with roughly 50% in foreign securities. Even a verbal signal of reallocation moved JGB yields down ~20bps intraday (July 10). A formal mandate change would be a UK 2022 LDI-style event for global bond markets (especially US Treasuries), and would simultaneously affect s1_basis_trade, s14_pensions, and s21_clearinghouses. This is a genuine indicator gap that deserves explicit tracking as a leading warning variable.

japanese-investors-sold-29-6-billion-of-.headline: Japanese investors sold $29.6 billion of US Treasuries in Q1 2026 alone as domestic yields rise
japanese-investors-sold-29-6-billion-of-.summary: As JGB yields hit multi-decade highs, Japanese investors repatriated capital at an accelerating pace, selling $29.6 billion of US debt in Q1 2026—removing a historically reliable buyer from a market already facing large US fiscal deficits and elevated Treasury supply under the 'One Big Beautiful Bill' fiscal expansion.

proposed_indicator.label: Japanese net US Treasury sales (rolling 12-month, $bn)
proposed_indicator.rubric: Positive = net buying (supportive); negative = net selling (stress). Alert threshold: > -$50bn/quarter. Current Q1 2026: -$29.6bn.

japanese-investors-sold-29-6-billion-of-.rationale: Japan is the largest foreign holder of US Treasuries (~$1.1 trillion). Accelerating repatriation as domestic JGB yields become attractive directly tightens US financial conditions and is not captured by any existing s2_japan indicator (which focuses on USD/JPY, 30y JGB yield, and Takaichi fiscal clarity). This is the transmission channel between Japan's fiscal/BOJ story and the US basis trade / Treasury market vulnerability (s1_basis_trade). The $29.6bn Q1 figure, annualised, would be the largest Japanese net sale of US Treasuries on record. A new indicator in s2_japan is warranted.

us-cpi-at-4-2-yoy-may-2026-with-new-fed-.headline: US CPI at 4.2% YoY (May 2026) with new Fed Chair Warsh dropping forward guidance; Fed funds floor at 3.50% as oil shock re-accelerates inflation
us-cpi-at-4-2-yoy-may-2026-with-new-fed-.summary: US CPI printed 4.2% YoY in May 2026—the highest 12-month reading since April 2023—driven by Middle East energy disruption. New Fed Chair Kevin Warsh eliminated traditional forward guidance in favour of pure data-dependence after the June meeting, raising uncertainty about the policy path precisely as a renewed US-Iran flare-up (July 8) sends oil back above $75–78/b, threatening a second inflation impulse.

proposed_story.name: Fed credibility / inflation resurgence trap
proposed_story.why: The US is entering a second inflationary impulse (energy shock re-ignition post-ceasefire collapse) with a new Fed chair who has removed forward guidance and a policy rate still at 3.50–3.75%—well below the May CPI of 4.2%. This is a distinct cross-cutting systemic risk not cleanly housed in any existing story: it tightens financial conditions for s3_private_credit (default rate risk), pressures s11_consumer_debt (real-income squeeze with subprime already stressed), amplifies s4_dev_debt (dollar strength + higher-for-longer), and undermines the goldilocks assumption embedded in current IG/HY spreads (s8_ai_capex, s3_private_credit). The removal of forward guidance introduces non-linear policy-shock risk.

indicators.label: US CPI YoY (%)
indicators.rubric: Trend matters: sustained >3.5% with energy re-acceleration = elevated; >4.5% = crisis signal. Current: 4.2% (May 2026).

indicators.label: Fed forward guidance clarity (0 clear / 1 data-dependent / 2 incoherent)
indicators.rubric: 0 = explicit rate path provided; 1 = data-dependent, no forward guidance (current post-Warsh); 2 = contradictory signals or surprise policy reversal.

indicators.label: US 2y–10y Treasury spread (bps)
indicators.rubric: Re-inversion (< 0 bps) signals recession-rate-hike collision. Steepening with inflation = stagflation regime. Current: modestly positive.

us-cpi-at-4-2-yoy-may-2026-with-new-fed-.rationale: No existing story cleanly captures the combination of (a) an entrenched US inflation resurgence above 4%, (b) a leadership change at the Fed that has dismantled forward guidance, and (c) a renewed energy price shock from Hormuz re-closure that threatens a second inflation impulse. This is distinct from s1_basis_trade (financial plumbing), s11_consumer_debt (household-level), and s4_dev_debt (EM-focused). The Warsh forward-guidance removal is a structural shift in US monetary policy communication that creates non-linear tightening risk and deserves its own story node. Materiality is medium-high given the potential to reprice rate expectations globally.

jgb-10-year-yield-hits-30-year-high-at-2.headline: JGB 10-year yield hits 30-year high at 2.90%; 30-year at ~3.96%; Japan GPIF rebalancing floated then walked back, stoking disorderly signal
jgb-10-year-yield-hits-30-year-high-at-2.summary: Japan's benchmark 10-year JGB yield touched 2.901% on July 9—its highest since 1996—while the 30-year remained near 3.96%, on mounting fiscal-expansion anxiety after the June 30 'Basic Policy shock' draft policy blueprint. Finance Minister Katayama's hint at GPIF rebalancing briefly calmed markets but was quickly walked back, compounding the credibility gap around Takaichi-plan financing.
jgb-10-year-yield-hits-30-year-high-at-2.target_indicator: 30-year JGB yield (%) AND Takaichi plan financing clarity
jgb-10-year-yield-hits-30-year-high-at-2.rationale: The 10y JGB hit a 30-year high and the 30y is pinned near 4%; both the yield level and the botched GPIF trial-balloon directly update the two key s2 indicators (30y JGB yield and Takaichi financing clarity, which should move to '2 disorderly'). The Basic Policy shock also revealed the government's attempt to implicitly constrain BOJ rate hikes, a qualitative deterioration in financing clarity. Over 70 bps of 10y yield rise year-to-date and a botched GPIF communication warrant a high-materiality flag.

japan-capital-repatriation-29-6bn-us-tre.headline: Japan capital repatriation: $29.6bn US Treasuries sold in Q1 2026 alone; TD Economics estimates 20–50 bps upward pressure on US 10y yield
japan-capital-repatriation-29-6bn-us-tre.summary: As domestic JGB yields reached multi-decade highs, Japanese investors—including banks, insurers, and government-related funds—sold a net $29.6 billion in US Treasuries in Q1 2026, with Ministry of Finance data showing ¥4 trillion in net foreign-security sales since January. TD Economics estimates this repatriation dynamic could add 20–50 basis points to US 10-year yields in the medium term, representing a structural withdrawal from the world's largest sovereign bond market at a time of near-$2 trillion annual US fiscal deficits.

proposed_indicator.label: Japan net UST holdings change (rolling-quarter, $bn)
proposed_indicator.rubric: Measures rolling-quarter net Japanese purchases (+) or sales (–) of US Treasuries per US TIC / Japan MOF data. Values more negative than –$20bn/quarter signal structural repatriation; flag at –$30bn, alarm at –$50bn. Complements the JGB yield indicator by capturing the cross-market transmission channel to US term premia.

japan-capital-repatriation-29-6bn-us-tre.rationale: The existing s2 indicators track JPY/USD, JGB yields, and Takaichi financing clarity, but none captures the feedback loop into US Treasury demand—the most systemically dangerous transmission path. Japan is the largest single foreign holder of US Treasuries (~$1.2 trillion), and a Q1 2026 sale of $29.6bn is already the fastest pace in years. An orderly continuation adds 20–50 bps to US 10y yields (TD Economics); a disorderly unwind could spike MOVE, stress basis trades (s1), and tighten financial conditions broadly. A dedicated indicator for Japan's net UST position is the missing link in the current tracker.

china-june-ppi-4-1-yoy-4-year-high-drive.headline: China June PPI +4.1% YoY (4-year high) driven by Iran energy shock—but MoM turned negative and consumer goods PPI still -0.9%, revealing a dangerous bifurcation
china-june-ppi-4-1-yoy-4-year-high-drive.summary: China's June PPI hit +4.1% year-on-year, the strongest since July 2022, primarily from Iran-war energy and commodity pass-through (mining PPI +16.5% YoY, raw materials +8.6%), while consumer goods PPI fell -0.9% YoY and the monthly reading turned negative (-0.3%) for the first time since July 2025. This cost-push / domestic-deflation split is the most adverse configuration for China's margins and export pricing power, simultaneously pressuring corporate profits and preventing consumer reflation.
china-june-ppi-4-1-yoy-4-year-high-drive.target_indicator: China PPI YoY (%) - large swing either way is worse
china-june-ppi-4-1-yoy-4-year-high-drive.rationale: A +4.1% PPI YoY is a large positive swing that the s5 indicator explicitly flags as adverse 'either way'. The specific mechanism—energy cost-push that is not passing through to consumers, compressing margins—is the China deflation spillover story's most dangerous configuration: exporters face margin squeeze and may intensify price dumping abroad (anti-dumping actions risk) while domestic demand remains anaemic (CPI 1.0%, below consensus). The MoM reversal also signals the energy impulse may be peaking, meaning PPI could whipsaw back negative if Hormuz normalises, creating a second adverse swing. Medium materiality because the driver (Hormuz) is now re-escalating.

japan-10-year-jgb-yield-touches-2-901-30.headline: Japan 10-year JGB Yield Touches 2.901% (30-Year High) on Takaichi Fiscal Shock; BOJ Independence Language Revised
japan-10-year-jgb-yield-touches-2-901-30.summary: The 10-year JGB yield spiked to 2.901% on July 10—its highest since 1996—driven by the government's June 30 'Basic Policy shock' blueprint (¥370 trillion investment roadmap) that markets read as an attempt to cap BOJ rate hikes. Finance Minister Katayama's GPIF reallocation signal triggered a partial retreat to ~2.78%, but yields are still 70+ bps higher year-to-date and Japanese investors sold $29.6bn of U.S. Treasuries in Q1 2026 alone.

proposed_indicator.label: Japanese investor net US Treasury sales (rolling 12-month, $bn)
proposed_indicator.rubric: 0 = net buying or < $20bn selling; 1 = $20–60bn selling (repatriation signal); 2 = > $60bn selling (disorderly repatriation, systemic Treasury supply shock)

japan-10-year-jgb-yield-touches-2-901-30.rationale: The existing s2_japan story tracks USD/JPY, the 30-year JGB yield, and Takaichi financing clarity. But the week's most novel sub-signal is quantified Japanese repatriation of US Treasuries ($29.6bn in Q1 alone). This is a direct feedback loop to s1_basis_trade and s21_clearinghouses: if Japanese life insurers and GPIF continue rotating from UST to JGBs, it adds idiosyncratic supply pressure to the long end of the US curve on top of existing fiscal deficits. No current indicator captures this bilateral flow. The 'Takaichi plan financing clarity' ordinal is the closest proxy but is too broad; a dedicated flow metric is needed.

section-122-tariffs-expire-july-24-ustr-.headline: Section 122 Tariffs Expire July 24; USTR Section 301 Replacement (No Rate Cap, No Expiry) Due July 20
section-122-tariffs-expire-july-24-ustr-.summary: The 10% broad-based Section 122 surcharge expires by statute on July 24, 2026, and cannot be extended unilaterally. USTR's replacement—Section 301 tariffs of 10–12.5% covering 46+ countries with no expiry date or rate cap—faces a completion deadline of July 20. China's Q2 GDP simultaneously missed Beijing's target (4.3% vs 4.5%–5% goal) for the first time since Covid, with property investment down 18% YoY.

proposed_indicator.label: US tariff-regime transition risk (ordinal)
proposed_indicator.rubric: 0 = stable regime (Section 301 finalized, rates known); 1 = transition uncertainty (Section 122 lapsing, Section 301 pending or contested in court); 2 = disorderly cliff (Section 122 expired, no Section 301 replacement, IEEPA injunction in force—effective tariff rate collapses or spikes unpredictably)
sources: Penn Wharton Budget Model tariff tracker: https://budgetmodel.wharton.upenn.edu

section-122-tariffs-expire-july-24-ustr-.rationale: The existing s5_china_deflation story tracks PPI, USD/CNY, and anti-dumping actions, but does not capture the imminent US tariff-regime cliff. The July 24 expiry of Section 122 with simultaneous Section 301 finalization is a discrete, time-stamped binary event with direct impact on China's export-dependent growth model (now exposed by the Q2 GDP miss) and on global supply-chain pricing. A milder tariff outcome would amplify China's deflationary export push; a harsher Section 301 outcome would reignite the PPI/anti-dumping cascade. The indicator is needed to flag transition-state risk that none of the three existing s5 indicators currently captures.

us-cpi-at-4-2-yoy-may-2026-new-fed-chair.headline: US CPI at 4.2% YoY (May 2026); New Fed Chair Warsh Drops Forward Guidance Amid Sticky Inflation and Middle East Shock
us-cpi-at-4-2-yoy-may-2026-new-fed-chair.summary: New Fed Chair Kevin Warsh removed traditional forward guidance after the June FOMC meeting, shifting to pure data dependence as US CPI hit 4.2% YoY in May 2026—the highest since April 2023—partly driven by Middle East energy costs. The Fed funds rate remains at 3.50–3.75%, and the policy pivot risk (toward hikes rather than cuts) is a new tail risk not previously priced for 2026.

proposed_story.name: Fed Policy Re-anchoring Risk (Warsh Era)
proposed_story.why: The appointment of Kevin Warsh as Fed Chair and his immediate removal of forward guidance represents a structural break in how markets price US monetary policy. With CPI at 4.2% YoY (a multi-year high), the Middle East energy shock keeping inflation sticky, and the new chair signaling pure data dependence, the tail risk of a return to rate hikes—rather than the cuts markets had priced—creates a cross-cutting shock vector that amplifies s1_basis_trade (leveraged Treasury positions get squeezed if rates rise unexpectedly), s3_private_credit (floating-rate direct lending defaults spike), s8_ai_capex (rate-sensitive capex financing), and s11_consumer_debt (delinquency acceleration). No existing story captures the Fed's institutional pivot itself as a standalone risk.

indicators.label: US CPI YoY (%)
indicators.rubric: Reading above 3.5% is elevated; above 4.5% is acute; above 5% triggers rate-hike pricing

indicators.label: Fed forward-guidance clarity (ordinal)
indicators.rubric: 0 = explicit forward guidance in place; 1 = data-dependent, no rate-path signal (current Warsh posture); 2 = active rate-hike bias or emergency meeting risk
sources: FOMC statement and press conference transcripts: https://www.federalreserve.gov/monetarypolicy/fomccalendars.htm

indicators.label: Fed funds futures implied rate 12-months forward (%)
indicators.rubric: Rapid upward repricing (>50bps in <4 weeks) signals market pricing a hike cycle; downward repricing signals cuts still priced

us-cpi-at-4-2-yoy-may-2026-new-fed-chair.rationale: No existing story in the tracker covers the Fed's institutional framework shift. The Warsh forward-guidance removal is not routine noise: it is a structural change in the monetary policy communication regime that arrived alongside the highest US CPI print since 2023. Combined with a Middle East energy shock keeping inflation sticky, this creates a plausible path to rate hikes in a tracker that currently models all monetary risk as 'cuts delayed.' The cross-story amplification effects (basis trade, private credit, AI capex, consumer debt) are all first-order, making this a new_risk warranting its own story rather than being absorbed into an existing indicator.

us-iran-strait-of-hormuz-ceasefire-colla.headline: US–Iran Strait of Hormuz ceasefire collapses; US re-launches strikes and naval blockade, transit drops to near-zero
us-iran-strait-of-hormuz-ceasefire-colla.summary: An interim US–Iran peace agreement signed in June broke down by ~July 11, 2026, with the US launching multiple rounds of air and naval strikes and Iran retaliating against US bases across the Gulf; MarineTraffic data show only ~13 vessels transited the Strait in a 24-hour window vs. ~110 pre-war, and Brent crude jumped 4% to ~$79/bbl on July 13. The IMF noted that the strategic crude-inventory buffer used in March–May has now been significantly drawn down, meaning a renewed supply shock has less cushion.
us-iran-strait-of-hormuz-ceasefire-colla.target_indicator: Hormuz/Red Sea status — update from '1 contested' to '2 closed/re-contested'; WTI crude (CL=F) spiking; also hits s18_food_fertilizer (Hormuz/Gulf disruption indicator) and s2_japan (energy-import shock channel)
us-iran-strait-of-hormuz-ceasefire-colla.rationale: The Strait of Hormuz is the single most material physical chokepoint in the tracker. The ceasefire collapse and renewed US naval blockade directly move s20's 'Hormuz/Red Sea status' indicator from 1→2 (effectively re-closed for commercial traffic), push WTI/Brent higher (oil indicator), and ripple into s18 (fertilizer inputs via Gulf) and s2_japan (Japan is a major energy importer, 10yr JGB yield already at 30yr highs partly on energy-import cost). The IMF's July 2026 WEO explicitly flags 'renewed Middle East conflict' as the dominant downside risk, and the strategic petroleum reserve buffer has been substantially depleted.

japan-10yr-jgb-hits-30-year-high-of-2-90.headline: Japan 10yr JGB hits 30-year high of 2.90%; Takaichi ¥370T spending roadmap unveiled; Japanese investors sold $29.6B of US Treasuries in Q1 2026
japan-10yr-jgb-hits-30-year-high-of-2-90.summary: Japan's 10-year JGB yield surged to 2.901% on July 9 (highest since 1996), with the 20-year touching 3.901%, driven by PM Takaichi's mid-July unveiling of a ¥370 trillion public-private investment roadmap and a 'Basic Policy shock' concern that the government might lean on the BOJ to pause hikes. Japan's Finance Minister separately signaled pressure on the $1.8 trillion GPIF to repatriate foreign assets, with Goldman Sachs estimating even in-band shifts could redirect ~$80bn from foreign bonds to JGBs.

proposed_indicator.label: Japanese investor net sales of US Treasuries (rolling 12-month, $bn)
proposed_indicator.rubric: Measures repatriation pressure on US Treasury demand. 0–$50bn = manageable; $50–$150bn = elevated (amber); >$150bn = systemic stress (red). Q1 2026 pace of $29.6bn/quarter implies ~$120bn annualized run-rate, borderline amber.

japan-10yr-jgb-hits-30-year-high-of-2-90.rationale: The existing s2_japan indicators (USD/JPY, 30y JGB yield, Takaichi plan financing clarity) do not directly capture Japanese capital repatriation away from US Treasuries, which is the transmission mechanism that makes Japanese fiscal stress a global macro event. Japanese investors sold $29.6bn of US debt in Q1 2026 alone; a potential GPIF in-band shift could add another ~$80bn. This is a distinct second-order risk to US Treasury market liquidity that sits between s2_japan and s1_basis_trade but is captured by neither. The 30y JGB yield indicator in s2 should also be updated: 30y has already breached 4% and the 'danger zone' threshold per market analysts is 4.5% (forced life-insurer selling).

iea-warns-china-s-rare-earth-controls-en.headline: IEA warns China's rare-earth controls endanger $6.5 trillion of Western downstream production; MOFCOM formalizes public-reporting enforcement mechanism effective July 1
iea-warns-china-s-rare-earth-controls-en.summary: On July 16, 2026 the IEA's Global Critical Minerals Outlook warned that full implementation of China's rare-earth export restrictions (delayed until November 2026) could put $6.5 trillion of automotive, high-tech, defence, and energy-sector production outside China at risk. Separately, MOFCOM Announcement No. 26 (effective July 1, 2026) formalized a whistleblower mechanism for violations, and Japanese nationals were detained in Dalian in May for alleged rare-earth smuggling — the first known such case involving foreign nationals.
iea-warns-china-s-rare-earth-controls-en.target_indicator: Nov-2025 pause status — with full implementation now less than 4 months away (November 2026) and active enforcement ratcheting up (MOFCOM No.26 + detentions), move from '0 extended' toward '1 expiring'; US domestic RE producer blacklist — MP Materials and USA Rare Earth were added to China's export control list in June 2026, update to '1 entity-specific ban'; Ex-China heavy rare-earth price premium — upward pressure confirmed by IEA and CSIS data
iea-warns-china-s-rare-earth-controls-en.rationale: Multiple s6_rare_earths indicators move simultaneously this week: (1) the November 2026 expiry of the pause is now <4 months away and the IEA's $6.5T headline quantifies the systemic exposure; (2) MOFCOM No. 26's whistleblower mechanism signals enforcement intent, not just regulatory architecture; (3) detention of Japanese nationals represents a qualitative escalation to extraterritorial coercion; (4) MP Materials and USA Rare Earth entity-listing in June directly triggers the 'US domestic RE producer blacklist' indicator. Collectively these developments move the story from amber to near-red.

genius-act-rulemaking-deadline-hits-july.headline: GENIUS Act rulemaking deadline hits July 18, 2026 with final implementing rules not yet issued — foreign and state issuers face legal limbo
genius-act-rulemaking-deadline-hits-july.summary: The GENIUS Act (signed July 18, 2025) required regulators to finalize stablecoin access rules within one year; as of July 17, 2026, OCC, Treasury/FinCEN, and FDIC proposals remain in proposed — not final — form, leaving foreign issuers' equivalency status and state-qualified issuers' compliance posture unresolved. The Act's effective date triggers either 120 days after final rules or January 18, 2027, meaning a rulemaking slip pushes market clarity further out and creates access uncertainty for ~$266bn in stablecoin supply (USDT ~$189bn, USDC ~$77bn).
genius-act-rulemaking-deadline-hits-july.target_indicator: GENIUS implementation friction — update from '0 smooth' to '1 gaps': the July 18 rulemaking deadline is being missed for final rules on foreign issuer equivalency and state-issuer compliance, creating a legal-access gap for a market with >$266bn in outstanding supply
genius-act-rulemaking-deadline-hits-july.rationale: The tracker's GENIUS implementation friction indicator was designed precisely for this scenario. The one-year rulemaking deadline is tomorrow (July 18) and final rules have not landed, meaning the friction score moves from 0→1. While not an immediate systemic shock, the limbo creates uncertainty for foreign stablecoin issuers (Tether is offshore) and for the Treasury-demand side of s7: if stablecoin supply growth stalls or issuers restructure reserves away from T-bills pending clarity, it removes an incremental source of T-bill demand at a moment when US fiscal supply is high.

imf-july-2026-weo-global-disinflation-ha.headline: IMF July 2026 WEO: Global disinflation has 'stalled'; financial-market repricing risk elevated; US CPI at 4.2% YoY (May 2026)
imf-july-2026-weo-global-disinflation-ha.summary: The IMF's July 8, 2026 World Economic Outlook Update stated that 'global disinflation has stalled' with global headline inflation projected to rise from 4.1% in 2025 to 4.7% in 2026, and flagged 'downside risks from renewed conflict and financial market repricing.' Separately, US CPI printed 4.2% YoY through May 2026 — the largest 12-month increase since April 2023 — with the Fed (under Chair Kevin Warsh) holding rates at 3.50–3.75% while removing forward guidance, and JP Morgan projecting 10yr yields at 4.7% by year-end.

proposed_indicator.label: US CPI YoY (%, monthly) as a proxy for Fed-hold duration / EM re-pricing pressure
proposed_indicator.rubric: Tracks whether sticky US inflation keeps the Fed on hold and tightens EM financial conditions. <3% = benign; 3–4% = elevated (amber); >4% = acute EM stress risk (red). Current reading: 4.2% (May 2026) = red. Cross-links to s4_dev_debt (EM capital outflow risk), s2_japan (BOJ divergence), s11_consumer_debt.

imf-july-2026-weo-global-disinflation-ha.rationale: The tracker's s4_dev_debt watches the broad US dollar index and 10yr real yield as EM stress proxies, but lacks a direct US-inflation indicator that captures the Fed-hold-for-longer channel. The IMF's explicit 'disinflation stalled' language and the 4.2% May CPI print under a new Fed chair who has removed forward guidance represent a qualitative regime shift: if the Fed is forced to pivot hawkish (or merely hold longer), EM FX, EM credit spreads, and frontier-debt coupons all come under renewed pressure — exactly the s4 transmission mechanism. This is a story_gap because no current indicator in s4 directly tracks US CPI.

japan-s-government-floats-gpif-reallocat.headline: Japan's government floats GPIF reallocation toward domestic JGBs; BOJ raised rates to 1% in June; yen stuck near ¥160/$ despite ¥11.7T in intervention
japan-s-government-floats-gpif-reallocat.summary: On July 10, Finance Minister Katayama proposed encouraging GPIF and other pension funds to invest more in Japanese assets, triggering a brief rally in JGBs before Reuters reported no immediate GPIF allocation change; separately the BOJ hiked rates 25bp to 1% (highest since 1995) on June 16 — split 7-1 — and is tapering JGB purchases from ¥2.7T/quarter to ¥2T/month by April 2027. The yen has languished near ¥160/$ despite ¥11.7T in intervention in May, and with the BOJ meeting on July 30–31, a surprise hike or guidance shift risks a sharp carry-trade unwind.
japan-s-government-floats-gpif-reallocat.target_indicator: Multiple s2 indicators move: USD/JPY near ¥160 (high risk level); 30yr JGB yield elevated (20yr hit 3.901% on July 10); Takaichi plan financing clarity deteriorating toward '1 unclear / 2 disorderly' given ¥370T roadmap market shock. July 30–31 BOJ meeting is the next acute risk event for carry-trade unwind (links to s1_basis_trade leverage).
japan-s-government-floats-gpif-reallocat.rationale: The BOJ rate hike to 1%, ongoing JGB tapering, yen weakness despite massive intervention, and the GPIF reallocation trial balloon all cluster into the s2_japan story this week. The July 30–31 BOJ meeting is a live event within the next 14 days that could trigger a carry-trade unwind at scale — the same channel that produced the August 2024 volatility episode. With USD/JPY near ¥160, 10yr JGB at 30-year highs, and Japanese pension funds beginning to repatriate US assets, the risk level across s2 indicators is the highest since tracking began.

jgb-10-year-yield-hits-2-90-a-30-year-hi.headline: JGB 10-Year Yield Hits 2.90%, a 30-Year High; Japanese Investors Sold $29.6bn of US Treasuries in Q1 2026
jgb-10-year-yield-hits-2-90-a-30-year-hi.summary: Japan's 10-year JGB yield touched 2.901% on July 10—its highest since 1996—as Takaichi administration fiscal expansion plans (¥370 trillion through 2040) and BOJ policy normalization (rate at 1.0% after June hike) collide with a weakening yen near 160 USD/JPY. Japanese investors sold $29.6bn of US debt in Q1 2026 alone, removing a key backstop buyer from US Treasury markets.

proposed_indicator.label: Japanese investor net US Treasury purchases (rolling quarter, $bn)
proposed_indicator.rubric: Positive = net buying (supportive); 0 to -15 = mild repatriation (watch); -15 to -30 = significant repatriation (elevated); below -30 = acute stress on US duration markets. Q1 2026 reading: -$29.6bn.

jgb-10-year-yield-hits-2-90-a-30-year-hi.rationale: The existing s2_japan story captures USD/JPY and 30-year JGB yield, but has no indicator tracking Japanese capital repatriation from US Treasuries, which is the direct transmission channel from Japan's domestic yield surge to US rate markets. At -$29.6bn in Q1 alone, this is already a high-materiality gap: it amplifies s1_basis_trade leverage stress and s8_ai_capex financing costs simultaneously.

us-supreme-court-struck-down-ieepa-tarif.headline: US Supreme Court Struck Down IEEPA Tariffs (Feb 2026); Administration Pivots to Section 301/232, Imposes 25% on Brazil Effective July 22
us-supreme-court-struck-down-ieepa-tarif.summary: The Supreme Court's February 2026 ruling (Learning Resources v. Trump) struck down IEEPA-based tariffs, forcing the administration to reconstruct its tariff wall via Section 301 and 232; Section 122 authority also faces court challenge and expires July 24. The US announced 25% tariffs on thousands of Brazilian imports (sugar, apparel, steel, paper) effective July 22—the first Section 301 action post-IEEPA—while new tariff rates were imposed on 14 additional countries including Japan and South Korea ahead of an August 1 deadline.

proposed_story.name: US Tariff Legal Architecture Fragility & Trade Escalation Cascade
proposed_story.why: The Supreme Court's invalidation of IEEPA tariffs and the ongoing legal challenge to Section 122 have forced the US to reconstruct its entire tariff framework under more legally constrained Section 301/232 authorities. This creates a volatile, rolling re-escalation risk: every country facing new Section 301 investigations can retaliate, bilateral trade deals remain in legal limbo, and $135bn+ in previously collected tariff revenue may be subject to refund litigation. The pivot to Brazil (first Section 301 action) and new rates on 14 countries signals this is systemic—not episodic—reshaping of global trade flows with direct pass-through to inflation, supply chains, and EM sovereign stress.

indicators.label: US average effective tariff rate (%, estimated)
indicators.rubric: Below 10% = low risk; 10–15% = elevated; 15–20% = high (current JPMorgan estimate: mid-to-high teens); above 20% = acute macro drag and inflation pass-through

indicators.label: Legal durability of tariff authority (0 stable / 1 challenged / 2 voided)
indicators.rubric: 0 = tariff authority upheld by courts; 1 = active appellate challenge or one authority struck; 2 = primary authority voided (as occurred with IEEPA Feb 2026) forcing abrupt policy reconstruction

indicators.label: Number of active Section 301 / 232 retaliatory investigations by trading partners
indicators.rubric: 0–5 = contained; 5–15 = escalating; 15+ = cascade risk to global trade volumes

us-supreme-court-struck-down-ieepa-tarif.rationale: No existing tracker story captures the systemic legal fragility of the US tariff architecture. This is distinct from s5_china_deflation (bilateral China tariffs) and s4_dev_debt (EM stress): it is a cross-cutting meta-risk where the mechanism used to set tariffs worldwide has been repeatedly invalidated by courts, forcing rolling ad hoc reconstructions that create unpredictable escalation nodes (Brazil, Japan, Germany pharma investigations) with global trade-flow and inflation consequences.

us-reimpos-s-hormuz-blockade-brent-surge.headline: US Reimposés Hormuz Blockade; Brent Surges Past $84 on Renewed US–Iran Strikes (Jul 14–15, 2026)
us-reimpos-s-hormuz-blockade-brent-surge.summary: After a fragile ceasefire briefly allowed oil prices to retreat toward pre-war levels, the US reimposed its naval blockade of Iranian ports on July 14, carrying out new strikes on Iranian missile, drone, and naval assets; Brent crude jumped ~9.5% in a single session to above $83–$85/bbl, with analysts warning of $100+ if hostilities persist. The IMF's July 2026 WEO baseline assumes Hormuz begins reopening by mid-July, making this re-escalation a direct downside shock to that baseline.
us-reimpos-s-hormuz-blockade-brent-surge.target_indicator: Hormuz/Red Sea status (0 open / 1 contested / 2 closed) → move to 2 (closed/blockaded); WTI crude (CL=F) → ~$79–$80/bbl as of Jul 15
us-reimpos-s-hormuz-blockade-brent-surge.rationale: The US military reimposition of the Hormuz blockade (Jul 14) and fresh airstrikes directly escalate s20's primary indicator from 'contested' to 'closed/blockaded.' The ~9.5% single-session Brent spike and analyst calls for $100+ materially move both the Hormuz status ordinal AND the WTI price indicator. This also feeds into s18_food_fertilizer (sulphur, fertilizer logistics disrupted per IEA) and s4_dev_debt (energy-importer EMDE stress), but the direct indicator match is s20.

japan-10-year-jgb-yield-hits-30-year-hig.headline: Japan 10-Year JGB Yield Hits 30-Year High at ~2.90%; 'Basic Policy Shock' Rattles Market; BoJ Independence Under Political Pressure (Jul 9–16, 2026)
japan-10-year-jgb-yield-hits-30-year-hig.summary: The benchmark 10-year JGB yield surged to 2.901%—its highest since 1996—on a combination of fiscal expansion fears under PM Takaichi's ¥370 trillion investment roadmap and a brief 'Basic Policy shock' in which markets feared the government would discourage BoJ rate hikes. Finance Minister Katayama's subsequent remarks and a report that Japan may encourage GPIF to shift toward domestic assets caused a partial pullback to ~2.73–2.78%, but the BoJ's political independence remains in question per Reuters (Jul 16).

proposed_indicator.label: BoJ political-independence risk (0 intact / 1 pressure / 2 override)
proposed_indicator.rubric: 0 = government affirms BoJ autonomy, no policy interference signals; 1 = credible reports of government pressure on rate-hike timing or bond-buying directives, or 'Basic Policy shock'-type episodes; 2 = formal instruction or legislation overriding BoJ rate decisions or mandating bond purchases above market-clearing levels.

japan-10-year-jgb-yield-hits-30-year-hig.rationale: The existing s2 indicators (USD/JPY, 30yr JGB yield, Takaichi financing clarity) partially capture this, but none tracks whether political actors are actively leaning on the BoJ's rate-setting autonomy—a distinct transmission channel. The 'Basic Policy shock' episode (market pricing in government interference with BoJ hikes) and the Reuters Jul 16 report of anticipated political pressure on bond-buying are new material developments in this week's window that fall in the story's gap. Current read: 1 (pressure).

iea-warns-china-s-rare-earth-curbs-endan.headline: IEA Warns China's Rare-Earth Curbs Endanger $6.5 Trillion of Downstream Production; MOFCOM Activates Public Violation-Reporting Mechanism (Jul 16 & Jul 1, 2026)
iea-warns-china-s-rare-earth-curbs-endan.summary: The IEA's latest Critical Minerals Outlook (published Jul 16) quantified that full implementation of China's rare-earth export restrictions could jeopardise $6.5 trillion in downstream production outside China, with the US and Europe accounting for nearly half the impact. Separately, MOFCOM Announcement No. 26 (effective Jul 1) formalised a public whistleblower mechanism for strategic mineral export-control violations, and Chinese enforcement detained two Japanese nationals in Dalian in May for alleged rare-earth smuggling—the first known foreign-national detentions under this framework.

proposed_indicator.label: China rare-earth enforcement intensity (0 administrative / 1 active prosecution / 2 foreign-national detentions + sector-wide reporting mandate)
proposed_indicator.rubric: 0 = licensing system operational, violations handled via administrative penalty; 1 = domestic Chinese exporter prosecutions and/or enforcement notices targeting specific entities; 2 = foreign-national detentions on smuggling/export-control charges AND/OR a formalised public-reporting mechanism (whistleblower system) for violations effective across all jurisdictions.

iea-warns-china-s-rare-earth-curbs-endan.rationale: The existing s6 indicators track license flow, pause status, US domestic producer blacklists, and ex-China price premium. None captures the enforcement posture—how aggressively China is prosecuting violations. The MOFCOM Jul 1 whistleblower activation and the first-ever foreign-national detention in a rare-earth enforcement action are qualitatively new escalations this week (current read: 2). The IEA $6.5T headline also materially upgrades the Nov-2025 pause expiry risk (now Nov 10, 2026, ~16 weeks away).

genius-act-misses-its-statutory-one-year.headline: GENIUS Act Misses Its Statutory One-Year Rulemaking Deadline (Jul 18, 2026); Final Rules Still Unwritten
genius-act-misses-its-statutory-one-year.summary: US federal regulators failed to finalise stablecoin implementing rules by the GENIUS Act's mandated July 18, 2026 deadline, with multiple AML, capital, and operational rule-packages still in comment or draft form. This locks the effective date to January 18, 2027 (the 18-month backstop), leaving stablecoin issuers planning around draft rules that could still change and creating a six-month implementation vacuum.
genius-act-misses-its-statutory-one-year.target_indicator: GENIUS implementation friction (0 smooth / 1 gaps / 2 problems) → move from 1 to 2 (statutory deadline missed; no final rules as of Jul 18, 2026; issuers face draft-rule uncertainty through Jan 2027)
genius-act-misses-its-statutory-one-year.rationale: The tracker already monitors GENIUS implementation friction with a 0/1/2 rubric. The failure of all six agencies to finalise rules by the statutory one-year deadline—confirmed by CoinDesk (Jul 19) and CoinPaprika (Jul 18)—is a clean, verifiable trigger for moving the indicator from 1 (gaps) to 2 (problems). The effective-date backstop shift to Jan 2027 prolongs legal uncertainty for issuers and Treasury-demand dynamics.

japan-gpif-signalled-to-shift-toward-dom.headline: Japan GPIF Signalled to Shift Toward Domestic Assets; USD/JPY Carry Unwind Accelerates (Jul 9–10, 2026)
japan-gpif-signalled-to-shift-toward-dom.summary: Reports that Japan's government is encouraging the GPIF—the world's largest pension fund at ~$1.5 trillion in AUM—to boost domestic investment sparked an acceleration of yen-buying and a JPY carry-trade unwind, with Japanese assets rallying sharply. Finance Minister Katayama's subsequent clarification that GPIF would 'adjust holdings if necessary' and include JGBs in a tax-free investment program partially contained but did not reverse the move.
japan-gpif-signalled-to-shift-toward-dom.target_indicator: USD/JPY (yen strengthening = lower = risk) — yen buying accelerated sharply on GPIF headlines; also 30-year JGB yield (%) — 20-year JGB hit 3.901% intraweek, 30-year trading near multi-decade highs; Takaichi plan financing clarity → 1 (unclear), given ¥370tn roadmap and GPIF pivot signals
japan-gpif-signalled-to-shift-toward-dom.rationale: The GPIF domestic-reallocation signal is a distinct carry-unwind catalyst captured by the existing USD/JPY and 30yr JGB yield indicators in s2_japan. It is highly material because GPIF holds an estimated $350–400bn in foreign bonds; even a modest reallocation would trigger significant cross-asset flows. The 20-year JGB reaching 3.901% and the 10-year touching 2.901% in the same week reinforce all three existing indicators simultaneously.

middle-east-energy-shock-stalls-global-d.headline: Middle East Energy Shock Stalls Global Disinflation; IMF Revises 2026 Headline Inflation Up to 4.7% (Jul 8, 2026)
middle-east-energy-shock-stalls-global-d.summary: The IMF's July 2026 World Economic Outlook Update revised global headline inflation up to 4.7% for 2026 (from 4.1% in 2025), explicitly citing the Middle East war-driven energy shock as the primary driver; the disinflation trend in place since 2024 has 'stalled.' The IMF baseline assumes Hormuz reopening begins mid-July—a baseline that has already been violated by the Jul 14 US blockade reimposition, implying the inflation forecast carries further upside risk.

proposed_indicator.label: War-energy inflation pass-through to EMDE sovereign stress (0 contained / 1 rising / 2 acute)
proposed_indicator.rubric: 0 = global headline inflation ≤4.0% and EMDE spreads stable; 1 = IMF or World Bank formally revises EMDE growth down AND global inflation above 4.0% with energy as primary driver, or capital outflows from ≥3 EMDEs; 2 = global headline inflation ≥5.5% OR ≥5 EMDEs face acute refinancing stress, currency crises, or missed coupons directly attributable to energy-cost pass-through.

middle-east-energy-shock-stalls-global-d.rationale: s4_dev_debt already tracks the dollar index, real yields, and new IMF programs/missed coupons. None captures the specific energy-shock inflation channel—the IMF WEO Jul 2026 explicitly flags this as the dominant new risk to EMDEs, with global inflation revised to 4.7% and disinflation described as 'stalled.' The World Bank separately projects EMDE growth at its weakest since the pandemic. An indicator that links Hormuz-driven energy inflation directly to EMDE sovereign stress (via tighter financial conditions and oil-import bills) fills this gap. Current read: 1 (rising).

strait-of-hormuz-actively-contested-iran.headline: Strait of Hormuz Actively Contested: Iran Strikes UAE Tankers, US Conducts Multi-Night Bombing Runs
strait-of-hormuz-actively-contested-iran.summary: As part of the ongoing 2026 Iran War (begun February 28), Iran struck two UAE supertankers in the Strait of Hormuz on July 13 while the US completed a third consecutive night of strikes on Iranian coastal defense, missile, and maritime capabilities. Brent crude surged ~9.6% in a single session to $83.30/bbl, its largest daily gain in over six years.
strait-of-hormuz-actively-contested-iran.target_indicator: Hormuz/Red Sea status → upgrade to 2 (contested/partially closed); WTI crude → ~$78/bbl (rising); Undersea cable incidents → 0 (no confirmed cable cut, but tanker strikes active)
strait-of-hormuz-actively-contested-iran.rationale: Iranian tanker strikes and US bombardment of Iranian maritime-defense infrastructure represent a direct kinetic threat to the Strait of Hormuz chokepoint (~20% of global oil and LNG). The single-day Brent +9.6% move and reimposition of an Iranian port blockade are precisely the triggers s20 was designed to track. The WTI indicator in s20 needs an immediate update (~$78/bbl on Jul 13 settle). This is the single highest-materiality event of the week and bleeds into s18 (fertilizer inputs via Gulf), s5 (China energy costs), and s4 (EM energy-import stress).

imf-july-weo-flags-stalled-global-disinf.headline: IMF July WEO Flags Stalled Global Disinflation; Raises 2026 Headline Inflation to 4.7%, Cites 'War Shock' Weighing on Energy Importers
imf-july-weo-flags-stalled-global-disinf.summary: The IMF's July 8, 2026 World Economic Outlook Update revised global headline inflation up to 4.7% for 2026 and noted the disinflation trend in place since early 2024 has stalled, driven largely by the 2026 Iran war energy shock. The World Bank separately flagged that EM/DEs face their weakest per-capita income growth since COVID.
imf-july-weo-flags-stalled-global-disinf.target_indicator: New IMF programs / missed coupons → rising to 1 (rising); US 10y real yield (TIPS) → watch for repricing as disinflation stalls; Broad US dollar index → monitor for EM pressure as energy-import bills rise
imf-july-weo-flags-stalled-global-disinf.rationale: Stalled global disinflation with upward-revised inflation (4.7%) directly pressures EM/developing-world debt sustainability: higher-for-longer DM rates reduce rollover capacity, and rising energy import bills compress fiscal space. The IMF WEO explicitly notes 'war shock weighing on energy importers and vulnerable economies.' This worsens the s4 indicator set — the IMF program / missed coupon ordinal should shift toward 1 (rising stress), and the real yield environment deserves a flag. The World Bank's weakest EMDE per-capita growth since COVID adds corroborating weight.

genius-act-turns-one-final-rules-miss-ju.headline: GENIUS Act Turns One: Final Rules Miss July 18 Statutory Deadline as Six Agencies Still Finalizing
genius-act-turns-one-final-rules-miss-ju.summary: The GENIUS Act's July 18, 2026 one-year rulemaking deadline has passed with six federal agencies (OCC, FDIC, NCUA, Treasury, FinCEN, OFAC) still in the process of finalizing implementing rules rather than having issued them — representing a slip against the statutory timetable. CoinDesk confirmed on July 19 that regulators are 'still working their way through the process,' leaving the $240bn+ stablecoin market in regulatory limbo ahead of the 120-day compliance clock.
genius-act-turns-one-final-rules-miss-ju.target_indicator: GENIUS implementation friction → upgrade from 0 (smooth) to 1 (gaps): the July 18 statutory rulemaking deadline has been missed, agencies have only issued NPRMs not final rules, creating compliance uncertainty for a $240bn market
genius-act-turns-one-final-rules-miss-ju.rationale: The s7_stablecoins story tracks GENIUS implementation friction explicitly. A confirmed slip past the July 18 statutory rulemaking deadline — with OCC issuing only a proposed (not final) rule and CoinDesk confirming agencies are still drafting — is exactly the '1 = gaps' condition. Total stablecoin supply ($240bn+, up from tracker's ~baseline) and the mandatory Treasury-reserve backing means this also has second-order implications for UST demand (relevant to s7's Treasury demand angle). The friction score should move from 0 to 1.

takaichi-370-trillion-blueprint-unveils-.headline: Takaichi '¥370 Trillion Blueprint' Unveils Massive Fiscal Expansion Across 17 Sectors; BOJ Independence Signal Muddled by 'Basic Policy Shock'
takaichi-370-trillion-blueprint-unveils-.summary: PM Takaichi's mid-July 2026 economic blueprint — committing over ¥370 trillion in public and private investment through fiscal 2040 — triggered a 'Basic Policy shock' in JGB markets when investors briefly feared the government would discourage future BOJ rate hikes. A Cabinet clarification on July 10 that BOJ autonomy 'must be respected' partially calmed markets, but the financing clarity of the plan remains deeply opaque.
takaichi-370-trillion-blueprint-unveils-.target_indicator: Takaichi plan financing clarity → upgrade to 2 (disorderly): ¥370tn blueprint with unclear financing, BOJ-independence ambiguity ('Basic Policy shock'), and 30y JGB already above 4% signal active market disorder around fiscal sustainability
takaichi-370-trillion-blueprint-unveils-.rationale: This is a direct hit on the s2_japan 'Takaichi plan financing clarity' indicator. The ¥370 trillion blueprint with no clear funding path, combined with a market-panicking signal that the government might suppress BOJ rate hikes (even if later clarified), pushes the ordinal indicator to 2 (disorderly). The 10y JGB at 2.90% (30-year high) and 30y JGB above 4% — with life-insurer forced-seller threshold at 4.5% — confirms material disorder. This is distinct from the GPIF repatriation entry above, which focuses on cross-border capital flow implications.

us-cpi-hits-4-2-yoy-may-2026-fed-under-n.headline: US CPI Hits 4.2% YoY (May 2026), Fed Under New Chair Warsh Removes Forward Guidance; Stagflation Risk from Energy Shock Intensifies
us-cpi-hits-4-2-yoy-may-2026-fed-under-n.summary: US CPI rose 4.2% year-over-year through May 2026 — the highest since April 2023 — while new Fed Chair Kevin Warsh eliminated traditional forward guidance in favor of pure data-dependence after the June meeting, leaving the funds rate at 3.50–3.75%. The combination of a war-driven energy shock, stalled disinflation, and opaque Fed signaling is increasing the probability of a stagflationary environment that tightens financial conditions globally.

proposed_story.name: US Stagflation Trap: Energy-Shock Inflation + Growth Deceleration + Fed Paralysis
proposed_story.why: US CPI at 4.2% YoY while the Fed sits at 3.50–3.75% with a new chair who has deliberately removed forward guidance creates a policy vacuum precisely when an exogenous energy shock (Iran war, Hormuz) is re-accelerating inflation. This is not captured by any existing story: s20 tracks oil/sea lanes physically, s8 tracks AI capex stress, s3/s9/s11 track credit and consumer stress — but none tracks the systemic feedback loop of a stagflationary US macro regime (high inflation + decelerating growth + Fed trapped between cutting [inflation] and hiking [growth]) that would simultaneously pressure EM debt (s4), private credit (s3), consumer debt (s11), and CRE (s9) while preventing the Fed from acting as a global lender of last resort.

indicators.label: US CPI YoY (%)
indicators.rubric: Current: 4.2% (May 2026). Threshold: <3% = easing; 3–4% = elevated; >4% = stagflation risk zone; >5% = acute. Higher is worse given simultaneous growth deceleration.

indicators.label: Fed forward-guidance opacity (0 = clear / 1 = ambiguous / 2 = none)
indicators.rubric: 0 = standard forward guidance in place; 1 = guidance qualified or conditional; 2 = guidance explicitly removed (pure data-dependence declared). Current: 2 (Warsh removed guidance June 2026).

indicators.label: US real GDP growth QoQ annualized (%)
indicators.rubric: Tracks growth leg of stagflation trap; <1% = deceleration concern; <0% = stagflation confirmed. Sources: BEA advance/revised GDP releases.

indicators.label: 5y5y inflation breakeven (%)
indicators.rubric: Measures whether inflation expectations are becoming un-anchored; >2.75% = elevated; >3.25% = de-anchoring risk. Sources: FRED T5YIFR.

us-cpi-hits-4-2-yoy-may-2026-fed-under-n.rationale: A US CPI at 4.2% with the Fed having just removed forward guidance and an exogenous energy-price shock still intensifying (Hormuz) constitutes a qualitatively new macro regime risk not tracked in the existing story set. The tracker has stories for specific asset-class stresses but lacks a 'meta-story' for the US macro policy trap that would amplify nearly every other story simultaneously. Proposed at level 6 because: (a) it is already partly in motion (CPI above 4%, guidance removed), (b) the energy shock adds a supply-side inflation driver the Fed cannot address with rate policy without crushing growth, and (c) under stagflation, the Fed's ability to pivot as a global backstop is severely constrained.
